pathophysiology_summary: An atopic (filaggrin/barrier-impaired) individual has a severe eczema flare triggered by cold, dry winter air, low humidity from heating and a recent viral URTI, producing ill-defined erythematous scaly flexural plaques with excoriation, micro-vesiculation, serous weeping and chronic lichenification, plus profound itch-driven sleep deprivation. The two must-not-miss complications are eczema herpeticum (widespread clustered monomorphic punched-out erosions/vesicles with fever/malaise — a dermatological emergency needing systemic aciclovir) and bacterial impetiginisation/staphylococcal superinfection (honey-coloured crusts, spreading warmth, fever); both are screened negative here (no punched-out erosions, no honey crust, afebrile). Discriminators: symmetrical flexural distribution with atopic history points to endogenous eczema over contact dermatitis; finger-web sparing and no burrows argue against scabies. Management fundamentals are liberal emollients, an adequately potent topical corticosteroid to the inflamed flexures, trigger reduction (humidify, lukewarm showers, moisturise), itch/sleep support, and — for a severe flare — a short course of oral steroids may be used cautiously (rebound risk), with a clear safety-net for herpetic/bacterial complications.

differential.evidence_basis: Flexural itchy scaly weeping plaques on a lifelong atopic background, winter/URTI trigger.
A widespread painful eczema flare must be screened for herpetic superinfection.
Weeping excoriated eczema commonly superinfects with Staph — screen for honey crust/spread/fever.
Intensely itchy but favours finger-webs/wrists with burrows, not flexural atopic pattern.
Generalised symmetrical flexural distribution with atopy favours endogenous eczema over a single external allergen.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnose an eczema flare and actively screen for eczema herpeticum and impetiginisation; give the fundamentals (emollient + topical steroid + triggers), not steroids alone.

differential.evidence_basis: Flexural atopic eczema; no herpetic/bacterial complication on screening.
No honey crust/spreading warmth/fever — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: emollients + topical steroid + trigger control + itch/sleep support; herpetic/bacterial safety-net; short oral steroid used cautiously.

red_flags.description: Eczema herpeticum: widespread clustered monomorphic punched-out erosions/vesicles with fever/malaise
red_flags.clinical_significance: A dermatological emergency requiring urgent systemic aciclovir; can be severe/disseminated.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed eczema herpeticum can disseminate and become life-threatening.

red_flags.description: Bacterial impetiginisation/staphylococcal superinfection (honey crusts, spreading warmth, fever)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Common complication of broken eczematous skin needing antibiotics.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening a severe painful flare for herpetic superinfection.
Prescribing steroids but omitting liberal emollients and trigger control (the mainstay of eczema management).
Reaching for oral steroids as routine, risking rebound flares, rather than optimising topical therapy.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Close inspection for punched-out monomorphic erosions
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prescribe emollients + adequately potent topical corticosteroid, trigger control and itch/sleep support; urgent review/ED if herpetic (punched-out sores + fever) or bacterial (honey crust/spread/fever) complications develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing eczema herpeticum, or treating with steroids alone without emollients/trigger control.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Atopic dermatitis: liberal emollients plus a topical corticosteroid of potency matched to severity/site; identify triggers; treat secondary infection; recognise eczema herpeticum as an emergency.
